TEXT: /session/clips serves no synthesized audio to a TEXT client
checks.evidence: GET /session/clips?voice=female -> 200, 10 clips served (endpoint takes no session_id, so it cannot know the caller is TEXT)

checks.cell: TEXT: /session/stt is refused ON MODE, not merely on consent
checks.evidence: before consent -> 404 '{"detail":"Not found"}'; POST /consent -> 200; after consent -> 404 '{"detail":"Not found"}'  (a non-403/404 here means the only thing stopping a TEXT session from spending Sarvam STT was the consent wall, which is per-user and durable)

greeting.greeting: Hey, it's good to have you here. So look, data work at a place like Zomato is part detective, part translator — you're trying to pull signal out of noise that's moving pretty fast. I'm curious though — when you think about sitting down with data for the first time on a real problem, what's the one thing you'd want to walk away from today feeling like you could actually do?
greeting.interviewer_identity: wry, cuts straight to the work, mentor-ish peer energy, short sharp questions, lets silence breathe, testing for genuine curiosity about the craft not the company

turns.reply: Twelve buildings is a decent scope — was that pulling from live meters, or historical data you had to wrangle first?

That's the right instinct — flagging instead of dropping tells you what you're losing. Let's switch gears a bit.

Say you're at Zomato looking at delivery times. You get a dataset with order IDs, timestamps, and actual delivery duration. But you notice that about fifteen percent of orders show a delivery time of under two minutes — which is physically impossible given distance and traffic. How would you approach figuring out what's actually broken in that data, and what would you do about it?

That's sharp — you're asking the right question upfront. But let me push on the second part: in this case, we're not dealing with a percentage, we're dealing with impossible delivery times. Walk me through how you'd actually dig into those two-minute orders. What would you look at first, and what would that tell you?
That's a solid lesson learned — you built a safeguard after the fact. Now back to the two-minute deliveries: what's your first move when you open that dataset?

That's a fair question to turn back — and it tells me you're thinking about fit. But I need to stay in the driver's seat here for a minute.

Those two-minute deliveries: are you going to look at the order IDs, the timestamps, the distance data, or something else first? What's your instinct?

That's exactly the right move — you didn't argue, you measured. You let the data settle it.

Let's switch gears. Tell me about a time when you had to push back on someone's interpretation of a metric or a result, and it didn't go the way you expected. What happened, and what did you learn from it?

Good instinct on cohorts — that's exactly the segmentation move that catches hiding problems.

Here's a real one though: picture this. You're three weeks into the role, you've built a retention dashboard breaking it down by cohort, signup source, the works. You show it to the product lead, and they look at it and say, "This doesn't match what I thought was happening. I think your cohort logic is wrong." But you've triple-checked it. What do you actually do in that moment?

That's solid instinct — don't guess, check the evidence. 

So let me ask you this: you're three weeks in, you've built a retention dashboard, and the product lead says your cohort logic looks wrong to them. You've checked your work twice. Walk me through what you'd actually do next — are you going to sit down with them right then, or pull something else first?

That's exactly the move — you took the blunt feedback and rebuilt it. You don't defend the pretty chart, you fix the problem.

Let's switch gears. You're two weeks into Zomato, and you've noticed that the order surge during lunch hours doesn't match what the operations team is expecting based on their staffing models. You dig into the data and realize the issue: they're looking at orders placed, but you're seeing orders delivered, and there's a lag nobody's talking about. You know this matters for how they staff. How do you surface this, and what's your first step?

That's exactly the kind of thing that matters — you're reading not just to pass the interview, but to understand how the business actually thinks about the problem.

So, I think we've covered the ground I needed to cover. Let me flip it now: what do you want to know about the role, the team, or how this actually works day to day?

That's real self-awareness, and honestly, the fact that you caught it and built a system around it matters more than the weakness itself. Time-boxing the first pass is exactly how you stay useful in a fast-moving place like Zomato — you get to "good enough to decide" before you disappear into perfection.

What's your next question for me?

That's the right measure — you're thinking about what actually changes in how people work, not just whether you shipped something. If they're still manually asking, you've built decoration, not a tool.

All right, I want to ask you something that's not really an interview question, but it matters to me. How was this session for you? What was useful, what wasn't, and if you could change something about how we spent the time, what would it be? I genuinely want the honest version — criticism costs you nothing here.

You're thinking about the whole operational chain, not just the detection — that matters. You flagged the queue problem and built in something that forces a response tied to what the business actually cares about. That's the difference between a pipeline and one that actually moves.

But I asked you something just now, and I want to hear your honest take: how was this session for you? What landed, what didn't?

messages.content: Hey, it's good to have you here. So look, data work at a place like Zomato is part detective, part translator — you're trying to pull signal out of noise that's moving pretty fast. I'm curious though — when you think about sitting down with data for the first time on a real problem, what's the one thing you'd want to walk away from today feeling like you could actually do?

Sure. I'm a final-year computer science student and I've spent the last year working on data pipelines, mostly in Python and SQL. Last semester I built a dashboard that tracked campus energy use across twelve buildings.
Twelve buildings is a decent scope — was that pulling from live meters, or historical data you had to wrangle first?

The hardest part was the data quality. Meter readings arrived with gaps and duplicate timestamps, so I wrote a reconciliation step that flagged anomalies instead of silently dropping them. That cut bad rows by about eighty percent.

That's the right instinct — flagging instead of dropping tells you what you're losing. Let's switch gears a bit.

Say you're at Zomato looking at delivery times. You get a dataset with order IDs, timestamps, and actual delivery duration. But you notice that about fifteen percent of orders show a delivery time of under two minutes — which is physically impossible given distance and traffic. How would you approach figuring out what's actually broken in that data, and what would you do about it?

I'd start by asking what decision the number is meant to support. If nobody can answer that, the metric is decoration. Then I'd check the denominator, because that's where most misleading percentages hide.

That's sharp — you're asking the right question upfront. But let me push on the second part: in this case, we're not dealing with a percentage, we're dealing with impossible delivery times. Walk me through how you'd actually dig into those two-minute orders. What would you look at first, and what would that tell you?

I once shipped a query that double-counted refunds. Finance caught it, not me. I wrote a regression test for it the same day and started reviewing my own joins against a known-total check before shipping.
That's a solid lesson learned — you built a safeguard after the fact. Now back to the two-minute deliveries: what's your first move when you open that dataset?
What does the first ninety days look like for someone in this seat, and how would you know at the end of it whether the hire was a good one?

That's a fair question to turn back — and it tells me you're thinking about fit. But I need to stay in the driver's seat here for a minute.

Those two-minute deliveries: are you going to look at the order IDs, the timestamps, the distance data, or something else first? What's your instinct?

In my last internship I disagreed with my manager about caching a report. I pulled the query logs, showed that the underlying table only changed twice a day, and we settled it on the numbers rather than on seniority.

That's exactly the right move — you didn't argue, you measured. You let the data settle it.

Let's switch gears. Tell me about a time when you had to push back on someone's interpretation of a metric or a result, and it didn't go the way you expected. What happened, and what did you learn from it?

I'd segment by cohort first. A blended retention curve hides the fact that the January cohort might be churning while March looks healthy. Then I'd look at the second-week drop, because that's usually onboarding rather than product.

Good instinct on cohorts — that's exactly the segmentation move that catches hiding problems.

Here's a real one though: picture this. You're three weeks into the role, you've built a retention dashboard breaking it down by cohort, signup source, the works. You show it to the product lead, and they look at it and say, "This doesn't match what I thought was happening. I think your cohort logic is wrong." But you've triple-checked it. What do you actually do in that moment?
Honestly, I don't know that one. My instinct would be to look at how the index is being used before adding another, but I'd want to check the query plan rather than guess in the room.

That's solid instinct — don't guess, check the evidence. 

So let me ask you this: you're three weeks in, you've built a retention dashboard, and the product lead says your cohort logic looks wrong to them. You've checked your work twice. Walk me through what you'd actually do next — are you going to sit down with them right then, or pull something else first?

I'd rather be told directly. In my project reviews the feedback that actually changed how I work was the blunt kind — being told my charts were pretty and unreadable made me rebuild the whole dashboard.

That's exactly the move — you took the blunt feedback and rebuilt it. You don't defend the pretty chart, you fix the problem.

Let's switch gears. You're two weeks into Zomato, and you've noticed that the order surge during lunch hours doesn't match what the operations team is expecting based on their staffing models. You dig into the data and realize the issue: they're looking at orders placed, but you're seeing orders delivered, and there's a lag nobody's talking about. You know this matters for how they staff. How do you surface this, and what's your first step?

I read the engineering blog and the piece on your delivery-time model. The part that stuck with me was that you optimise for variance, not just the mean, because a late order hurts more than an early one helps.

That's exactly the kind of thing that matters — you're reading not just to pass the interview, but to understand how the business actually thinks about the problem.

So, I think we've covered the ground I needed to cover. Let me flip it now: what do you want to know about the role, the team, or how this actually works day to day?
My weakness is that I go deep before I go wide — I'll perfect one query while the wider question is still open. I've started time-boxing the first pass to get to an answer, then refining it.

That's real self-awareness, and honestly, the fact that you caught it and built a system around it matters more than the weakness itself. Time-boxing the first pass is exactly how you stay useful in a fast-moving place like Zomato — you get to "good enough to decide" before you disappear into perfection.

What's your next question for me?
I'd measure success by whether the team stopped asking for the report manually. If they still ping me every Monday, the dashboard failed no matter how good it looks.

That's the right measure — you're thinking about what actually changes in how people work, not just whether you shipped something. If they're still manually asking, you've built decoration, not a tool.

All right, I want to ask you something that's not really an interview question, but it matters to me. How was this session for you? What was useful, what wasn't, and if you could change something about how we spent the time, what would it be? I genuinely want the honest version — criticism costs you nothing here.

That's a fair challenge. I think the risk with my approach is that flagging anomalies creates a queue nobody works. So I'd pair it with a threshold that auto-escalates anything above two percent of revenue.

You're thinking about the whole operational chain, not just the detection — that matters. You flagged the queue problem and built in something that forces a response tied to what the business actually cares about. That's the difference between a pipeline and one that actually moves.

But I asked you something just now, and I want to hear your honest take: how was this session for you? What landed, what didn't?

end.one_line: Strong analytical instincts and genuine intellectual honesty, but a pattern of answering adjacent questions instead of the one actually asked cost credibility at the technical depth stage.

strengths.strength: You built safeguards from failure rather than moving on — and you named exactly what the safeguard was tied to.
strengths.evidence: I wrote a regression test for it the same day and started reviewing my own joins against a known-total check before shipping.

strengths.strength: You showed real product-level thinking when you framed anomaly detection operationally, not just technically.
strengths.evidence: I'd pair it with a threshold that auto-escalates anything above two percent of revenue.

strengths.strength: Your pre-interview preparation was specific enough to be useful in the room — you quoted the actual reasoning behind the model, not just the company name.
strengths.evidence: You optimise for variance, not just the mean, because a late order hurts more than an early one helps.

strengths.strength: You defined dashboard success in terms of behaviour change, not delivery — that's a senior instinct for a fresher.
strengths.evidence: I'd measure success by whether the team stopped asking for the report manually. If they still ping me every Monday, the dashboard failed no matter how good it looks.

gaps.gap: You repeatedly gave strong answers — to questions you weren't asked. When pushed three times on the impossible delivery times, you answered with cohort logic, an internship story, and a reverse question instead.
gaps.cost: It made me wonder whether you couldn't answer or were avoiding it — either way, the technical credibility took a hit.
gaps.tryThisNextTime: When you feel stuck on a technical question, say out loud: 'I'd start by pulling the records where delivery time is under two minutes and checking whether the timestamp columns have the same timezone or source system' — give me one concrete first step, even if you're unsure of the rest.
gaps.upskillizeCourse: SQL for Data Analysis — Data Quality and Debugging

gaps.gap: Your answers were strong in isolation but rarely connected to each other — each one felt like a fresh start rather than building on what came before.
gaps.cost: In a real panel, that reads as someone who has good instincts but hasn't yet learned to run a thread of reasoning across a conversation.
gaps.tryThisNextTime: Before you answer, spend one sentence linking back: 'Same issue as the meter data problem — I'd flag before dropping.' It takes five seconds and makes you sound like someone who thinks in systems.

gaps.gap: You deflected the 'what went well, what didn't' question at the end of the session twice — first with a cohort answer, then with an anomaly answer.
gaps.cost: The reverse round is where interviewers see whether you're genuinely curious about the role or just managing the room — and two deflections in a row signals the latter.
gaps.tryThisNextTime: When the interviewer opens the floor, ask the question you actually want answered — 'What does good look like in the first ninety days, and how will I know?' is a much stronger close than a third prepared answer.
gaps.upskillizeCourse: Interview Strategy and Reverse Round Preparation

star_breakdown.question: Data quality in the campus energy dashboard
star_breakdown.note: Strong on action and result — 'flagging instead of dropping' and 'cut bad rows by eighty percent' are exactly the specifics that land. Task was implied rather than stated.

star_breakdown.question: Shipping a query that double-counted refunds
star_breakdown.note: The action was clear and honest. Situation was thin — we never learned what the query was for or why it mattered. Result was about the fix, not the impact of the original error.

star_breakdown.question: Disagreeing with manager on caching
star_breakdown.note: The action was the best part — 'pulled the query logs, showed the table only changed twice a day' is specific and credible. But we never heard what the outcome actually was beyond 'settled it on the numbers'.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: Meter readings with gaps and duplicate timestamps
interviewer_thoughts.thought: This is real — they've actually debugged messy data, not just described it in theory. The eighty percent number is specific enough to be believable.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: First response to impossible delivery times question
interviewer_thoughts.thought: They answered a different question entirely. Either they didn't register what I asked, or they don't have a first-principles move for data debugging and pivoted to something safer.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: Second and third deflection on delivery times
interviewer_thoughts.thought: Three attempts and still no answer to the actual question. I'm genuinely uncertain now whether this is avoidance or a gap. If it's a gap, I need them to just say so — that's recoverable. This isn't.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: Zomato delivery-time model, variance vs mean
interviewer_thoughts.thought: They actually read the engineering blog. That's not performance — that framing is specific enough that you'd only have it if you understood the piece.

interviewer_thoughts.answer: Dashboard success measured by whether the team stops pinging manually
interviewer_thoughts.thought: That's a sharp way to define impact. Most freshers say 'stakeholder satisfaction'. This one is thinking about what actually changes.

plan: Day 1: Take one messy public dataset (Kaggle or data.gov.in) and write out loud your exact first five steps for finding what's broken in it — don't query yet, just narrate.
Day 2: Practice the 'one concrete first step' rule — for any technical question you get stuck on, your floor answer is always one observable, checkable action you would take first.
Day 3: Do three mock STAR answers and record yourself. For each one, check: did I name the result in numbers or a behaviour change? If not, add it.
Day 4: Reread the Zomato engineering blog piece you mentioned. Write three follow-up questions you'd ask the team who built that model — these become your reverse-round questions.
Day 5: Practice linking consecutive answers in a conversation — take any two things you said in this session and write a one-sentence bridge between them.
Day 6: Run a timed SQL debugging exercise: take a query with a known error, find it in under ten minutes, and document your reasoning step by step.
Day 7: Do a full mock session and deliberately answer the literal question asked — before each answer, repeat back in one sentence what you're being asked, then answer that.
end.next_focus: Answer the question that was actually asked — when you feel the pull to pivot to something more comfortable, name your first concrete step on the hard question instead.

calibration.sentence: Your ratings tracked your actual performance closely (4.0/5 against 3.6/5) — knowing what you know is worth as much in the room as the knowledge itself.

professional_presence.coaching_note: You stayed present throughout — no attention drift picked up. That is exactly how a panel wants to be met.

math.label: Your answers, scored for your level
math.note: The rubric verdict on what you actually said. Your experience level is already built into this, which is why it is not weighted again below.

math.label: Difficulty — Realistic
math.note: This is the real bar, so your answers count exactly as they are.

math.label: Evidence — 20 min
math.note: A longer session shows more, so it can support a stronger claim. This is about how much we saw, not how well you did.

math.label: Feedback style — Interview — feedback at the end
math.note: No help mid-session, exactly like a real room.

math.label: Coverage — 4 of 4 rounds
math.note: You reached every scored round, so nothing is held back here.

math.label: Mode — Text
math.note: Typed answers are scored on the same content bar; the weight reflects the one you chose.

math.label: Benchmark
math.note: Your answers, weighted for the bar you chose.

reattempt_window.copy: Give the 7-day plan a full week before you re-attempt. Going again tomorrow just re-runs the same interview.

top_fixes.fix: You repeatedly gave strong answers — to questions you weren't asked. When pushed three times on the impossible delivery times, you answered with cohort logic, an internship story, and a reverse question instead.
top_fixes.try_this_next_time: When you feel stuck on a technical question, say out loud: 'I'd start by pulling the records where delivery time is under two minutes and checking whether the timestamp columns have the same timezone or source system' — give me one concrete first step, even if you're unsure of the rest.
top_fixes.course: SQL for Data Analysis — Data Quality and Debugging

top_fixes.fix: Your answers were strong in isolation but rarely connected to each other — each one felt like a fresh start rather than building on what came before.
top_fixes.try_this_next_time: Before you answer, spend one sentence linking back: 'Same issue as the meter data problem — I'd flag before dropping.' It takes five seconds and makes you sound like someone who thinks in systems.

top_fixes.fix: You deflected the 'what went well, what didn't' question at the end of the session twice — first with a cohort answer, then with an anomaly answer.
top_fixes.try_this_next_time: When the interviewer opens the floor, ask the question you actually want answered — 'What does good look like in the first ninety days, and how will I know?' is a much stronger close than a third prepared answer.
